last_item: Band-1.5: restore-test EXECUTION — Manager.RestoreTest runs `pgbackrest verify` (option a), handler wired, fake-Runner unit tests, honest UI copy
last_result: done — handleRestoreTest was a stub returning 'not implemented' so restore-test surfacing could only show 'never'. DESIGN-FIRST decision: chose option (a) `pgbackrest verify` (read-only repo+WAL checksum integrity, no restore) over scratch-restore options — the security/safety tie-break winner: never touches the live data dir, needs NO disk-headroom precheck or scratch cleanup, cannot itself cause data loss; smallest slice that removes the most risk. Manager.RestoreTest + VerifyCmd: verifies (not claims) ownership like Restore's read side (foreign owner = HARD STOP), records pass/fail restore_tests row on a DETACHED ctx (survives shutdown), labels the verified backup. Handler wired. Fake-Runner unit tests: success NEVER invokes backup/restore + records success; failure records fail row; fail row persists on cancelled ctx; foreign-owner hard-stop; invalid-stanza. Reviewer (feature-dev:code-reviewer) flagged the success banner overclaiming 'proven recoverable' → fixed to 'Your backup repository is verified intact' + Callout describes the checksum check not a full restore (UI text now says what verify actually does). Full Go gate + web typecheck/33 tests/build green.
notes: Band 1.5 (data durability) NEARLY DONE. Done: (1) 'last good backup was N ago' surfaced. (2) backup-failed/backup-stale rules + metrics + Collector.enrichBackup. (3) telemetry+alert evaluation LOOP wired (background.go). (4) SCHEDULED BACKUPS RUN: full+incremental cron jobs; Manager.backupMu single-flight = CodeConflict skip on overlap (no fail row/false alert). (5) off-host S3 nudge test-locked. (6) restore-test SURFACING (restoreTestStatus + RestoreTestStatus banner). (7) ***restore-test EXECUTION DONE: Manager.RestoreTest runs `pgbackrest verify` (VerifyCmd, command.go verifyTimeout=2h); handler wired; recordRestoreTest helper mirrors recordBackup; verify is READ-ONLY so it does NOT take backupMu (correct — won't corrupt). verify ≠ full restore, so verified_rows stays 0 (honest).*** REMAINING 1.5: only the DEEP-proof follow-up (option c — non-destructive scratch restore + boot + REAL row count into verified_rows; catches recovery-time failures verify can't: WAL-replay gaps, corrupt pg_control). That one DOES need a disk-headroom precheck + guaranteed scratch cleanup + never-touch-live-dir + integration-tagged proof — its own backlog item, do NOT rush. After that, band 1.5 is complete → move to band 2 (stability). Optionally wire OTLP exporter (nil in collector). Plumbing: alert engine reads Snapshot FIELDS via alert/metrics.go metricValue. Backups: internal/backup/manager.go (Backup takes backupMu.TryLock→ownership→terminal fail/success; RestoreTest verifyForRead→VerifyCmd→pass/fail row); internal/store/backup.go (ListBackups/ListRestoreTests newest-first, LatestSuccessfulBackup, InsertRestoreTest). Frontend SUCCESS_RESULTS={success,ok,completed,pass}/FAILURE_RESULTS={fail,failed,error} in Backups.tsx — Manager writes 'success'/'fail'. Scheduler: internal/scheduler — empty spec disables. go/psql/pg_ctl snap-sandboxed (run sandbox-disabled). TMPDIR empty in some shells — use /tmp.
north_star: An indie hacker can stand up and run a rock-solid Postgres entirely from the indiepg UI, on best defaults, with safe optional overrides.
